answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: The three scenarios analysed were 1.1°C (being the baseline scenario), a 1.5°C scenario and a 3.0°C scenario (all being relative to pre-industrial levels through to the end of the century)... we analysed our organisation's climate-related impacts and risks and assessed these against the three climate scenarios – Baseline, Orderly, and Hothouse World

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.1°C (Baseline), 1.5°C (Orderly), and 3.0°C (Hothouse World), all relative to pre-industrial levels through to the end of the century. No temperature overshoot is mentioned.
answers.evidence: The three scenarios analysed were 1.1°C (being the baseline scenario), a 1.5°C scenario and a 3.0°C scenario (all being relative to pre-industrial levels through to the end of the century).

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple data sources are named: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), NIWA, Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS), International Energy Agency (IEA), Climate Change Commission (CCC), Ministry for the Environment (MfE), and Stats NZ. The scenario development was facilitated by Tonkin + Taylor, in collaboration with a health sector working group led by Te Whatu Ora.
answers.evidence: Sources of data include but are not limited to: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, NIWA, Network for Greening the Financial System, International Energy Agency and Climate Change Commission, Ministry for the Environment and Stats NZ. ... Radius Care was a participant in the health sector working group led by Te Whatu Ora and a collective group of health sector stakeholders within New Zealand who collaborated to develop a specific set of climate change scenarios. The project drew on insights from public agencies, private sector entities, research institutes and academia, with Tonkin + Taylor facilitating the process.
answers.location_hint: Scenario Overviews, p.9-10; Scenario Analysis, p.7
answers.notes: No single scenario provider; scenarios were co-developed using multiple sources, facilitated by Tonkin + Taylor.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.notes: The document does not reference SSP or RCP identifiers at all. Scenarios are described by temperature outcome (1.1°C, 1.5°C, 3.0°C) and narrative labels (Baseline, Orderly, Hothouse World).

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: Radius Care was a participant in the health sector working group led by Te Whatu Ora and a collective group of health sector stakeholders within New Zealand who collaborated to develop a specific set of climate change scenarios. The project drew on insights from public agencies, private sector entities, research institutes and academia, with Tonkin + Taylor facilitating the process. We then worked with our own external experts through a series of workshops to further develop the company's own scenarios relevant to our organisation. ... Physical risks were adapted from the Ministry for the Environment's Arotakenga Huringa Āhuarangi: A Framework for the National Climate Change Risk Assessment for Aotearoa New Zealand. The list was supplemented with transitional risks adapted from both XRB and TCFD guidance.
answers.notes: Scenarios were adapted for the New Zealand health/aged-care sector context, drawing on multiple sources and further developed through workshops with external experts.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: Radius Care was a participant in the health sector working group led by Te Whatu Ora and a collective group of health sector stakeholders within New Zealand who collaborated to develop a specific set of climate change scenarios. The project drew on insights from public agencies, private sector entities, research institutes and academia, with Tonkin + Taylor facilitating the process. We then worked with our own external experts through a series of workshops to further develop the company's own scenarios relevant to our organisation.
answers.notes: The scenario development involved a collaborative, multi-stakeholder process (health sector working group, public agencies, private sector, research institutes, academia) facilitated by Tonkin + Taylor, consistent with co-production.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes. The Baseline (1.1°C) scenario is described as the reference scenario representing current policy settings and current levels of warming, against which the Orderly (1.5°C) and Hothouse (3.0°C) scenarios are compared.
answers.evidence: This is our baseline assessment of current policy settings and impacts of 1.1°C of warming based on our current assessment of physical and transitional risks as they relate to a New Zealand context. This scenario assumes an orderly and immediate transition to a low emission economy and the impact of these actions limits increases to global warming to current levels. ... A baseline risk assessment representing 1.1°C of global warming above pre-industrial levels; A scenario risk assessment of two further scenarios representing 1.5°C and 3.0°C of global warming above pre-industrial levels.
answers.notes: The Baseline (1.1°C) scenario explicitly represents current policy settings and is used as the reference point for the other two scenarios.

answers.question: Model used
answers.notes: No specific climate models, integrated assessment models (IAMs), or macroeconomic models are named in the document. Data sources (IPCC, NIWA, NGFS, IEA, CCC, MfE, Stats NZ) are cited but no named models.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Increased storminess and extreme winds... Potential delays and disruption to supply chains similar to those experienced during the Auckland floods and Cyclone Gabrielle. Increased damage to buildings and properties susceptible to extreme storms and wind such as roofs, gardens and outdoor furniture. Reduced communications and/or power outages during extreme weather events... Examples of the impacts include one off costs following significant weather events and replacement/repair costs for some assets affected during these events.
answers.location_hint: Risks and Opportunities table, p.11-12; Financial Impacts, p.13
answers.notes: Damage and loss from acute physical risks (storms, floods, extreme weather) are explicitly included in the scenario analysis, though quantified financial estimates are not yet disclosed for future anticipated impacts (Adoption Provision 2 exemption applies). Historical one-off costs from weather events are referenced.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: Extreme Rainfall Days (per annum) ... Extreme Heat (>25°C) Days (per annum) ... Sea Level Rises ... Carbon Price ... Population Increases ... Renewable Energy ... Seasonal climate patterns change leading to increases in storm intensity and river flooding. Seasonal climate patterns change leading to increases in drought severity, and fire weather. Socio-political instability is low/high.
answers.location_hint: Scenario Overviews, p.9-10; Scenario descriptions, p.8

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: The climate risk assessment was predominantly limited to New Zealand and focused on the location, logistics and health impacts expected to be encountered... Sources of data include but are not limited to: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, NIWA, Network for Greening the Financial System, International Energy Agency and Climate Change Commission, Ministry for the Environment and Stats NZ... NZ - 2.8% Global - 0.8% Population Increases... NZ - 16% Global - 7% Population Increases
answers.location_hint: Scenario Analysis p.7; Scenario Overviews p.9-10
answers.notes: The scenarios draw on global data sources (IPCC, NGFS, IEA) but are applied in a New Zealand context with NZ-specific variables (NZ carbon price, NZ rainfall data from Stats NZ, NZ population figures). The document does not explicitly use the term "downscaled" but the application is clearly localised to New Zealand.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short term: 1–10 years; Medium term: 10–30 years; Long term: over 30 years. A brief rationale is provided, linking these time horizons to strategic asset development and growth plans. The overall scenario analysis covers risks emerging between 2023 and 2050.
answers.evidence: Short term is considered a one to ten year time frame, medium term is from ten to thirty years, and long term is anything over thirty years. These time horizons are of particular relevance when we are assessing the strategic development of our assets and our strategic growth plans.
answers.location_hint: Time Horizons, p.10; Risk Management, p.16-17
answers.notes: A brief rationale is given (relevance to strategic asset development and growth plans), but no deeper explanation of why these specific cut-off years were chosen.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: Radius Care has adopted a range of scenarios which offer a very optimistic, optimistic and pessimistic view on plausible futures. Using these scenarios provides Radius Care with a current view of resilience having captured recent experiences and impacts from a changing climate. The pessimistic future view provides Radius Care with a scenario, that when included in developing future strategy, will help to create a resilient approach... Using a combination of scenarios is intended to add resilience to the risk assessment process and resultant strategic decisions, preparing Radius Care for inevitable uncertainty in the short to medium-term.
answers.location_hint: Description of Scenarios, p.8; Scenario Overviews, p.9-10
answers.notes: The justification is qualitative and high-level — the three scenarios were chosen to represent optimistic, moderate, and pessimistic futures to build resilience and prepare for uncertainty. No technical justification for the specific temperature levels or data sources is provided.
